⌂ Hub MATHS-II · LEARN & PRACTICE
U1U2U3U4U5
BS 201 MT · Semester 2 · a slow, step-by-step guide

Mathematics – II
learn it, then practise it

Two big engines of applied maths: linear algebra (matrices) and differential equations. This guide breaks each unit into small lessons, explains the idea in plain words, then builds up with examples and practice you can check yourself.

How to use this page. Differential equations reward pattern-recognition: most of the skill is spotting which type of equation you're looking at, because each type has a fixed recipe. Learn to classify first, solve second.

Unit I

Matrices

Packaging linear systems into grids of numbers — and pulling out their hidden structure with rank and eigenvalues.

1.1 Rank & echelon form1.2 Solving systems1.3 Eigenvalues & vectors1.4 Cayley–Hamilton1.5 Practice
Lesson 1.1

Rank and echelon form

In plain words

A matrix is just a grid of numbers. Its rank is the number of genuinely independent rows — rows that aren't just copies or combinations of others. Rank tells you how much real information the system carries.

To find the rank, use row operations to reach echelon form (a staircase of zeros in the bottom-left), then count the non-zero rows.

Method — find the rank

  1. Use row operations (\(R_2\to R_2-2R_1\), etc.) to create zeros below each leading entry.
  2. Keep going until it's a "staircase" — echelon form.
  3. The rank is the number of rows that are not entirely zero.

Worked example 1.1a standard

Find the rank of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\2&4&6\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}\).

\(R_2\to R_2-2R_1\) Row 2 becomes \((0,\ 0,\ 0)\).

\(R_3\to R_3-R_1\) Row 3 becomes \((0,\ -1,\ -2)\).

Echelon form \(\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\\0&-1&-2\\0&0&0\end{pmatrix}\) — two non-zero rows.

Conclude Rank \(=2\).

Lesson 1.2

Solving systems of linear equations

The idea

A system like \(2x+y=5,\ x-y=1\) can be written as a matrix. Comparing the rank of the coefficient matrix with the rank of the "augmented" matrix (coefficients + right-hand side) tells you whether solutions exist, and how many.

Consistency rule

Let \(r\) = rank of coefficient matrix, \(R\) = rank of augmented matrix, \(n\) = number of unknowns.

\(r\ne R\): no solution.   \(r=R=n\): unique solution.   \(r=Rinfinitely many.

Worked example 1.2a warm-up

Solve \(x+y=3,\ 2x-y=0\).

Add cleverly Adding the equations: \(3x=3\Rightarrow x=1\).

Back-substitute \(1+y=3\Rightarrow y=2\).

Conclusion Unique solution \((x,y)=(1,2)\).

Lesson 1.3

Eigenvalues and eigenvectors

The idea

Most vectors, when multiplied by a matrix \(A\), get rotated and stretched. But a few special directions only get stretched (or squashed), not rotated. Those directions are the eigenvectors, and the stretch factor for each is its eigenvalue \(\lambda\).

Method — find eigenvalues and eigenvectors

  1. Solve the characteristic equation \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\) for the eigenvalues \(\lambda\).
  2. For each \(\lambda\), solve \((A-\lambda I)\mathbf x=\mathbf 0\) to find its eigenvector \(\mathbf x\).
  3. Check: eigenvalues should sum to the trace of \(A\), and multiply to \(\det A\).

Worked example 1.3a full problem

Find the eigenvalues and one eigenvector of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}4&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}\).

Characteristic equation \(\det\!\begin{pmatrix}4-\lambda&1\\2&3-\lambda\end{pmatrix}=(4-\lambda)(3-\lambda)-2=0\).

Expand & solve \(\lambda^2-7\lambda+10=0\Rightarrow(\lambda-2)(\lambda-5)=0\Rightarrow\lambda=2,\ 5\).

Check Sum \(=7=\) trace ✓; product \(=10=\det A\) ✓.

Eigenvector for \(\lambda=2\) \((A-2I)\mathbf x=\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}\mathbf x=\mathbf 0\Rightarrow 2x_1+x_2=0\). Choose \(x_1=1,\ x_2=-2\).

Result Eigenvector \((1,-2)\) for \(\lambda=2\).

Lesson 1.4

The Cayley–Hamilton theorem

The idea

Every matrix obeys its own characteristic equation. So if \(A\) has characteristic equation \(\lambda^2-7\lambda+10=0\), then \(A^2-7A+10I=0\). This is a shortcut to find \(A^{-1}\) and high powers of \(A\) without heavy computation.

Worked example 1.4a using the theorem

For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}\), verify Cayley–Hamilton and find \(A^{-1}\).

Characteristic equation \(\det(A-\lambda I)=(1-\lambda)^2-4=\lambda^2-2\lambda-3=0\).

Theorem says \(A^2-2A-3I=0\).

Verify \(A^2=\begin{pmatrix}5&4\\4&5\end{pmatrix}\), and \(A^2-2A-3I=\begin{pmatrix}5&4\\4&5\end{pmatrix}-\begin{pmatrix}2&4\\4&2\end{pmatrix}-\begin{pmatrix}3&0\\0&3\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\) ✓.

Find inverse From \(A^2-2A-3I=0\), multiply by \(A^{-1}\): \(A-2I-3A^{-1}=0\Rightarrow A^{-1}=\tfrac13(A-2I)=\tfrac13\begin{pmatrix}-1&2\\2&-1\end{pmatrix}\).

Unit I don't-lose-marks list
  • Always sanity-check eigenvalues against the trace and determinant.
  • An eigenvector is never zero — the whole point is a non-zero direction.
  • For Cayley–Hamilton inverse questions, rearrange the theorem to isolate \(A^{-1}\).

Practice — Unit I

For eigenvalue questions, form \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\) carefully — a sign slip here spoils everything.

Q1

Find the rank of \(\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&4\end{pmatrix}\).

SOLUTION \(R_2\to R_2-2R_1\) gives a zero row. One non-zero row remains.

Rank \(=1\).

Q2

Find the eigenvalues of \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&3\end{pmatrix}\).

SOLUTION Diagonal matrix — eigenvalues are the diagonal entries.

\(\lambda=2,\ 3\).

Q3

Find the eigenvalues of \(\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\).

SOLUTION Triangular — read the diagonal.

\(\lambda=3,\ 2\).

Q4

Find the eigenvalues of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}2&2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}\).

SOLUTION \((2-\lambda)^2-4=\lambda^2-4\lambda=0\Rightarrow\lambda(\lambda-4)=0\).

\(\lambda=0,\ 4\).

Unit II

Differential Equations — First Order

Equations that link \(y\) with its rate of change \(\tfrac{dy}{dx}\). The skill is recognising the type, because each type has a set recipe.

2.1 What an ODE is2.2 Separable2.3 Exact2.4 Linear & I.F.2.5 Bernoulli2.6 Practice
Lesson 2.1

What a differential equation is

In plain words

A differential equation connects an unknown function \(y\) with its derivatives. "Solving" it means finding the function \(y(x)\) that fits. The order is the highest derivative present — here we deal with first-order (only \(\tfrac{dy}{dx}\)).

The single most important habit: before solving, classify the equation. Is it separable? Exact? Linear? Bernoulli? Each label points to one method.

Lesson 2.2

Variables separable — the friendliest type

The idea

If you can get all the \(y\)'s (and \(dy\)) on one side and all the \(x\)'s (and \(dx\)) on the other, just integrate both sides separately. Done.

Worked example 2.2a warm-up

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=xy\).

Separate \(\dfrac{dy}{y}=x\,dx\).

Integrate both sides \(\ln|y|=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+C\).

Solve for \(y\) \(y=A\,e^{x^2/2}\) (where \(A=e^C\)).

Lesson 2.3

Exact equations

The idea

An equation \(M\,dx+N\,dy=0\) is exact if it came from differentiating some function \(F(x,y)\). The test is simple, and if it passes, you reconstruct \(F\).

Method — exact equations

  1. Test: is \(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}=\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\)? If yes, it's exact.
  2. Integrate \(M\) with respect to \(x\): \(F=\int M\,dx + g(y)\).
  3. Differentiate that \(F\) in \(y\), set equal to \(N\), and solve for \(g(y)\).
  4. Answer: \(F(x,y)=C\).

Worked example 2.3a standard

Solve \((2xy+3)\,dx+(x^2-1)\,dy=0\).

Test \(M=2xy+3,\ N=x^2-1\). \(M_y=2x,\ N_x=2x\) — equal, so exact ✓.

Integrate M in x \(F=\int(2xy+3)\,dx=x^2y+3x+g(y)\).

Match to N \(F_y=x^2+g'(y)=N=x^2-1\Rightarrow g'(y)=-1\Rightarrow g(y)=-y\).

Answer \(x^2y+3x-y=C\).

Lesson 2.4

Linear equations and the integrating factor

The idea

A linear first-order equation looks like \(\tfrac{dy}{dx}+P(x)\,y=Q(x)\). Multiply through by a cleverly chosen factor \(\mu\) and the left side collapses into a single derivative you can integrate.

The recipe

Integrating factor: \(\mu=e^{\int P\,dx}\). Then \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(\mu y)=\mu Q\), so \(\mu y=\int \mu Q\,dx + C\).

Worked example 2.4a standard

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+y=e^{x}\).

Identify \(P=1,\ Q=e^x\).

Integrating factor \(\mu=e^{\int 1\,dx}=e^x\).

Collapse the left side \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(y\,e^x)=e^x\cdot e^x=e^{2x}\).

Integrate \(y\,e^x=\dfrac{e^{2x}}{2}+C\Rightarrow y=\dfrac{e^x}{2}+Ce^{-x}\).

Lesson 2.5

Bernoulli's equation

The idea

A Bernoulli equation \(\tfrac{dy}{dx}+Py=Qy^{n}\) isn't linear because of the \(y^n\) — but a clever substitution \(v=y^{1-n}\) turns it into a linear equation you already know how to solve.

Worked example 2.5a substitution

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+y=y^{2}\).

Divide by \(y^2\) \(y^{-2}y'+y^{-1}=1\).

Substitute Let \(v=y^{-1}\), so \(v'=-y^{-2}y'\). The equation becomes \(-v'+v=1\), i.e. \(v'-v=-1\).

Solve the linear equation \(\mu=e^{-x}\): \((v e^{-x})'=-e^{-x}\Rightarrow v e^{-x}=e^{-x}+C\Rightarrow v=1+Ce^{x}\).

Back-substitute \(\dfrac1y=1+Ce^{x}\).

Classification cheat-sheet
  • Can you split \(x\)'s from \(y\)'s? → separable.
  • Form \(M\,dx+N\,dy=0\) with \(M_y=N_x\)? → exact.
  • Form \(y'+Py=Q\)? → linear (integrating factor).
  • Form \(y'+Py=Qy^n\)? → Bernoulli (substitute \(v=y^{1-n}\)).

Practice — Unit II

Name the type first, then apply its recipe.

Q1

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{y}{x}\).

SOLUTION Separable: \(\dfrac{dy}{y}=\dfrac{dx}{x}\Rightarrow\ln y=\ln x+C\).

\(y=Ax\).

Q2

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}+2y=4\).

SOLUTION Linear, \(P=2,\ Q=4\), \(\mu=e^{2x}\). \((ye^{2x})'=4e^{2x}\Rightarrow ye^{2x}=2e^{2x}+C\).

\(y=2+Ce^{-2x}\).

Q3

Is \((2x+y)\,dx+(x+2y)\,dy=0\) exact? If so, solve it.

SOLUTION \(M_y=1=N_x\) — exact. \(F=\int(2x+y)dx=x^2+xy+g(y)\); \(F_y=x+g'=x+2y\Rightarrow g=y^2\).

\(x^2+xy+y^2=C\).

Q4

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^{x-y}\).

SOLUTION \(e^{x-y}=e^x e^{-y}\), so separable: \(e^y\,dy=e^x\,dx\Rightarrow e^y=e^x+C\).

\(e^y=e^x+C\).

Unit III

Linear Differential Equations — Higher Order

Second-order and beyond. The full answer is always two pieces added together: the complementary function plus the particular integral.

3.1 CF + PI idea3.2 Auxiliary equation3.3 Particular integral3.4 Euler–Cauchy3.5 Practice
Lesson 3.1

The big picture: CF + PI

In plain words

For an equation like \(y''-5y'+6y=g(x)\), the full solution is two parts added:

the complementary function (CF) — the solution when the right side is zero, and the particular integral (PI) — one specific solution that matches the right side \(g(x)\). Answer \(=\) CF \(+\) PI.

If the right side is zero (a "homogeneous" equation), you only need the CF.

Lesson 3.2

The auxiliary equation and its three cases

The idea

Replace each \(\tfrac{d}{dx}\) with the letter \(m\). Solving the resulting polynomial (the auxiliary equation) gives roots that build the CF. There are exactly three cases, depending on the roots.

The three cases

Real & distinct \(m_1,m_2\):   \(y_c=c_1e^{m_1x}+c_2e^{m_2x}\).

Repeated root \(m\):   \(y_c=(c_1+c_2x)e^{mx}\)  (note the extra \(x\)).

Complex \(\alpha\pm i\beta\):   \(y_c=e^{\alpha x}(c_1\cos\beta x+c_2\sin\beta x)\).

Worked example 3.2a real roots

Solve \(y''-5y'+6y=0\).

Auxiliary equation \(m^2-5m+6=0\Rightarrow(m-2)(m-3)=0\Rightarrow m=2,3\).

CF (real distinct) \(y=c_1e^{2x}+c_2e^{3x}\).

Worked example 3.2b repeated & complex

Solve (i) \(y''-4y'+4y=0\); (ii) \(y''+4y=0\).

(i) Repeated root \(m^2-4m+4=(m-2)^2=0\Rightarrow m=2\) (twice). So \(y=(c_1+c_2x)e^{2x}\).

(ii) Complex roots \(m^2+4=0\Rightarrow m=\pm 2i\) (so \(\alpha=0,\beta=2\)). So \(y=c_1\cos 2x+c_2\sin 2x\).

Lesson 3.3

Finding the particular integral

The idea

When the right side isn't zero, guess a PI that "looks like" the right side (an \(e^{kx}\) for an exponential, \(a\cos+b\sin\) for a trig term), plug it in, and solve for the unknown constant.

Worked example 3.3a full CF + PI

Solve \(y''-3y'+2y=e^{3x}\).

CF \(m^2-3m+2=0\Rightarrow m=1,2\), so \(y_c=c_1e^{x}+c_2e^{2x}\).

Guess PI Try \(y_p=Ae^{3x}\). Then \(y_p'=3Ae^{3x},\ y_p''=9Ae^{3x}\).

Substitute \(9A-9A+2A=1\Rightarrow 2A=1\Rightarrow A=\tfrac12\). So \(y_p=\tfrac12 e^{3x}\).

Full solution \(y=c_1e^{x}+c_2e^{2x}+\tfrac12 e^{3x}\).

Watch out — the clash rule

If your guessed PI already appears in the CF, multiply your guess by \(x\) before substituting, otherwise it collapses to zero.

Lesson 3.4

Euler–Cauchy equations

The idea

An equation with \(x^2y'',\ xy'\) terms (variable coefficients) looks scary, but the substitution \(x=e^{t}\) magically turns it into a constant-coefficient equation you can already solve.

Euler–Cauchy form

\(x^2\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}+ax\dfrac{dy}{dx}+by=0\). Substitute \(x=e^{t}\) (so \(t=\ln x\)); it becomes constant-coefficient in \(t\).

Unit III don't-lose-marks list
  • Repeated root → don't forget the extra factor of \(x\).
  • Non-homogeneous → you need both CF and PI, added.
  • PI clashes with CF → multiply the trial by \(x\).

Practice — Unit III

Write the auxiliary equation clearly; the root type decides the CF form.

Q1

Solve \(y''-y=0\).

SOLUTION \(m^2-1=0\Rightarrow m=\pm1\) (real distinct).

\(y=c_1e^{x}+c_2e^{-x}\).

Q2

Solve \(y''+6y'+9y=0\).

SOLUTION \(m^2+6m+9=(m+3)^2=0\Rightarrow m=-3\) (repeated).

\(y=(c_1+c_2x)e^{-3x}\).

Q3

Solve \(y''+9y=0\).

SOLUTION \(m^2+9=0\Rightarrow m=\pm 3i\) (\(\alpha=0,\beta=3\)).

\(y=c_1\cos 3x+c_2\sin 3x\).

Q4

Find a PI for \(y''-y=e^{2x}\).

SOLUTION Try \(y_p=Ae^{2x}\): \(4A-A=1\Rightarrow 3A=1\Rightarrow A=\tfrac13\).

\(y_p=\tfrac13 e^{2x}\).

Unit IV

Series Solutions, Beta & Gamma

When coefficients aren't constant, assume the answer is an infinite polynomial. Plus two special functions that tame otherwise awkward integrals.

4.1 Power series solutions4.2 Frobenius method4.3 Gamma function4.4 Beta function4.5 Practice
Lesson 4.1

Power series solutions

The idea

Some differential equations have no neat formula solution. So we assume the solution is an infinite polynomial \(y=a_0+a_1x+a_2x^2+\cdots\), plug it in, and find the coefficients one by one by matching powers of \(x\).

A point where this works smoothly is called an ordinary point. Where it doesn't, we need a more careful method — Frobenius.

Lesson 4.2

The Frobenius method (near a singular point)

The idea

Near a "regular singular point", the ordinary power series fails. Frobenius' fix: assume \(y=x^{r}(a_0+a_1x+\cdots)\) with an unknown starting power \(r\). An indicial equation fixes \(r\), then a recurrence gives the coefficients.

You won't often be asked to derive it fully — but recognising when Frobenius (rather than a plain power series) is needed is the examinable skill.

Lesson 4.3

The Gamma function — factorials for all numbers

The idea

The Gamma function \(\Gamma\) extends the factorial to non-integers. For whole numbers it just gives the factorial; its real power is handling awkward integrals and half-integer values.

Key Gamma facts

\(\Gamma(n+1)=n\,\Gamma(n)\);   \(\Gamma(n+1)=n!\) for whole \(n\);   \(\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac12\right)=\sqrt{\pi}\).

Also: \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} x^{n-1}e^{-x}\,dx=\Gamma(n)\).

Worked example 4.3a warm-up

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} x^{3}e^{-x}\,dx\).

Match the form This is \(\Gamma(4)\) (since \(n-1=3\Rightarrow n=4\)).

Evaluate \(\Gamma(4)=3!=6\).

Lesson 4.4

The Beta function

Beta function \[B(m,n)=\int_0^1 x^{m-1}(1-x)^{n-1}\,dx=\frac{\Gamma(m)\,\Gamma(n)}{\Gamma(m+n)}\]

Worked example 4.4a standard

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x^{3}(1-x)^{2}\,dx\).

Read off m, n Exponents are \(m-1=3\Rightarrow m=4\) and \(n-1=2\Rightarrow n=3\). So it's \(B(4,3)\).

Apply the identity \(B(4,3)=\dfrac{\Gamma(4)\Gamma(3)}{\Gamma(7)}=\dfrac{3!\cdot 2!}{6!}=\dfrac{6\cdot 2}{720}=\dfrac{1}{60}\).

Remember
  • Beta's exponents are \(m-1\) and \(n-1\) — subtract one before reading off \(m,n\).
  • \(\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt\pi\) shows up whenever half-integers appear.

Practice — Unit IV

Match integrals to the Gamma or Beta template before computing.

Q1

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} x^{4}e^{-x}\,dx\).

SOLUTION \(=\Gamma(5)=4!\).

\(24\).

Q2

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x^{2}(1-x)^{3}\,dx\).

SOLUTION \(m=3,n=4\): \(B(3,4)=\dfrac{2!\,3!}{6!}=\dfrac{2\cdot6}{720}=\dfrac{1}{60}\).

\(\tfrac{1}{60}\).

Q3

Simplify \(\dfrac{\Gamma(6)}{\Gamma(4)}\).

SOLUTION \(\dfrac{5!}{3!}=\dfrac{120}{6}\).

\(20\).

Unit V

Special Functions — Legendre & Bessel

Two families of functions that appear whenever physics meets spherical or cylindrical shapes. You mostly use their properties rather than derive them.

5.1 Legendre polynomials5.2 Properties of \(P_n\)5.3 Bessel functions5.4 Practice
Lesson 5.1

Legendre polynomials and Rodrigue's formula

The idea

Legendre's differential equation produces a family of polynomials \(P_0,P_1,P_2,\dots\). There's a one-line machine for generating any of them: Rodrigue's formula.

Rodrigue's formula \[P_n(x)=\frac{1}{2^{n}\,n!}\frac{d^{n}}{dx^{n}}\left(x^2-1\right)^{n}\]

First few: \(P_0=1,\ P_1=x,\ P_2=\tfrac12(3x^2-1),\ P_3=\tfrac12(5x^3-3x)\).

Worked example 5.1a derive a polynomial

Find \(P_2(x)\) using Rodrigue's formula.

Set up (\(n=2\)) \(P_2=\dfrac{1}{2^2\cdot 2!}\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2}(x^2-1)^2=\dfrac{1}{8}\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2}(x^4-2x^2+1)\).

Differentiate twice First: \(4x^3-4x\). Second: \(12x^2-4\).

Simplify \(P_2=\dfrac{12x^2-4}{8}=\dfrac{3x^2-1}{2}\) ✓.

Lesson 5.2

Orthogonality of Legendre polynomials

The idea

Different Legendre polynomials are "perpendicular" to each other in an integral sense: multiplying two different ones and integrating over \([-1,1]\) gives zero. This is what makes them useful for expanding other functions.

Orthogonality \[\int_{-1}^{1}P_m(x)\,P_n(x)\,dx=0\quad(m\ne n)\]
Lesson 5.3

Bessel functions

The idea

Bessel's equation produces functions \(J_n(x)\) that behave like decaying oscillations — they describe vibrations of a circular drum, and waves in cylinders. Like Legendre's, they come with a handy recurrence relation.

Bessel recurrence relation \[J_{n-1}(x)+J_{n+1}(x)=\frac{2n}{x}\,J_n(x)\]
Unit V don't-lose-marks list
  • Keep the \(\tfrac{1}{2^n n!}\) prefactor in Rodrigue's formula.
  • Legendre orthogonality holds specifically over \([-1,1]\).
  • Bessel recurrence: memorise the \(\tfrac{2n}{x}\) factor.

Practice — Unit V

These are often "prove the property" questions — show every step.

Q1

Find \(P_1(x)\) using Rodrigue's formula.

SOLUTION \(P_1=\dfrac{1}{2^1\cdot1!}\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2-1)=\dfrac{1}{2}(2x)\).

\(P_1=x\).

Q2

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_{-1}^{1}P_1(x)\,P_2(x)\,dx\).

SOLUTION Different indices \((1\ne2)\), so by orthogonality it's zero.

\(0\).

Q3

Using the Bessel recurrence with \(n=1\), express \(J_0+J_2\) in terms of \(J_1\).

SOLUTION \(J_{0}+J_{2}=\dfrac{2(1)}{x}J_1\).

\(J_0+J_2=\dfrac{2}{x}J_1\).

Your weekly routine
  • Read one unit's lessons, working every example by hand.
  • Practise that unit with solutions hidden; re-do only the ones you missed.
  • Build a one-page formula sheet per unit (auxiliary-equation cases, integrating factors, Beta/Gamma).
  • Finish with matching questions from an Osmania University past paper.

About this page

A beginner-friendly, worked tutorial for Engineering Mathematics-II (BS 201 MT), BE Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering (Autonomous), Osmania University. Explanations, examples and practice are standard treatments aligned to the prescribed texts (Grewal; Jain & Iyengar; Raisinghania; Kreyszig). Equations render with MathJax; tap "Show solution" to check your practice answers.

Self-contained single file — upload index.html to tiiny.host to publish. Needs an internet connection to load web fonts and the MathJax renderer.  Compiled study reference, not an official university publication.  Prepared 04 July 2026.
